name: Wacker Polysilicon — Burghausen
why_it_matters: Europe's largest polysilicon site and one of five global producers capable of 11N semiconductor-grade purity; new €300 M Etching Line Next (2025) expands capacity >50%.

capacity.notes: Exact split of semi vs solar grade not disclosed; total site capacity expanded >50% in 2025 with new production line.

name: Wacker Polysilicon North America — Charleston
why_it_matters: Only large-scale semiconductor-grade polysilicon plant in the eastern US, reducing Western supply-chain exposure to non-allied producers; $2.5 B greenfield investment.

capacity.notes: Exact capacity not disclosed; one of the largest hyperpure polysilicon plants in North America. ~650 employees.

name: Hemlock Semiconductor — Hemlock MI
why_it_matters: Only US-owned hyper-pure polysilicon manufacturer; critical for domestic semiconductor supply chain; $325 M CHIPS Act award to expand capacity (new facility by 2028).

capacity.notes: Largest US poly producer; new facility adds significant incremental capacity by 2028. Total capex including CHIPS expansion ~$825 M.

name: Tokuyama Factory — Shunan (Yamaguchi)
why_it_matters: One of only five global producers of 11N (99.999999999%) semiconductor-grade polysilicon; ~20% global electronic-grade market share; integrated chlorine/hydrogen supply chain on site.

capacity.notes: 30,000 MT/yr at Shunan. Additional 8,000 MT/yr JV (OTSM) in Malaysia under construction.

name: OCI Holdings — Gunsan Polysilicon Plant
why_it_matters: Korea's sole dedicated semiconductor-grade polysilicon plant; 4,700 MT/yr of ultra-pure poly used by Korean and Taiwanese wafer makers; allied-bloc counterweight to Chinese dominance.

capacity.notes: Exclusively electronic-grade output. JV in Malaysia (OTSM with Tokuyama) will add 8,000 MT/yr by 2029.

name: Daqo New Energy — Xinjiang & Inner Mongolia
operator: Xinjiang Daqo New Energy Co., Ltd.
why_it_matters: One of the world's largest polysilicon producers at 305,000 MT nameplate capacity; dominant supplier to Chinese solar/semico supply chain; operates under US import restrictions due to Xinjiang sourcing.

capacity.notes: Xinjiang Daqo + Inner Mongolia 5A/5B facilities. Running at reduced utilization (40-50%) in 2024-25 due to low prices. ~70% N-type in 2024.

name: GCL Technology — Xinjiang Polysilicon Complex
why_it_matters: World's largest polysilicon producer at 480,000 MT capacity; controls ~17% of global supply; subsidiary XinHua Semiconductor holds >50% domestic semiconductor-grade market share.

capacity.notes: Includes granular silicon (FBR) and Siemens routes. XinHua Semiconductor unit: 10,000 MT/yr semiconductor-grade.

name: Sibelco — Spruce Pine HPQ Mine
why_it_matters: Produces ~70-90% of the world's high-purity quartz used for Czochralski crucibles; without HPQ, silicon ingot production halts — the ultimate upstream chokepoint for all semiconductor wafers.

capacity.notes: Spruce Pine district total ~180-200k MT/yr; Sibelco is the dominant producer. Numbers are estimates as industry is highly secretive.

name: The Quartz Corp — Spruce Pine Mine
why_it_matters: Second-largest Spruce Pine HPQ producer; together with Sibelco supplies nearly all global semiconductor-grade crucible quartz; Hurricane Helene 2024 forced temporary shutdown highlighting systemic fragility.

capacity.notes: Smaller than Sibelco's Spruce Pine operation; precise capacity undisclosed. District-wide output ~200k MT/yr.

name: Ferroglobe — Beverly (Globe Metallurgical)
why_it_matters: Ferroglobe controls 14% of global silicon metal capacity (ex-China) and 66% of North American capacity; Beverly is its primary US silicon and ferrosilicon plant, feedstock for polysilicon and silicone industries.

capacity.notes: Ferroglobe total Western World capacity ~420,000 MT/yr silicon metal. Beverly shares in this. Brownfield expansion planned for 2028.

name: Elkem Silicon Products — Salten
why_it_matters: One of Europe's largest silicon plants producing Silgrain® high-purity Si and specialty FeSi; integrated with Elkem's Norwegian quartz mines; key feedstock for Wacker Burghausen polysilicon.

capacity.notes: 3 furnaces, 1 energy recovery plant; silicon capacity 80,000 MT/yr.

ownership: Public (Elkem ASA listed on Oslo Børs; majority owned by China National Bluestar)

name: Aluminum Corporation of China (Chalco) — Gallium Refining
why_it_matters: China controls 99% of global primary gallium production; Chalco is the dominant state-owned producer; export controls since 2023 (and ban to US since 2024) constitute a direct semiconductor supply-chain weapon.

capacity.unit: % of global primary gallium output (China total)
capacity.notes: China produced 98% of world low-purity gallium in 2024 per USGS. Chalco is the primary state actor. Export ban to US since Dec 2024.

name: Neo Performance Materials — Silmet (Sillamäe)
why_it_matters: Only commercial rare earth separations and rare metal processing facility in the EU; produces NdPr, Dy, Tb oxides plus recycled gallium/indium — the sole non-Chinese allied-bloc RE separation node at scale.

capacity.notes: New heavy REE SX line commissioned 2026 for Dy and Tb. Sintered NdFeB magnet plant in Narva Estonia 2,000 t/yr initial capacity.

name: MP Materials — Mountain Pass
why_it_matters: Only scaled and operational rare earth mine and processing facility in the US; multibillion-dollar DoD partnership (2025) to build 10X magnet plant; critical for NdFeB magnets in AI-era motors/robotics.

capacity.notes: ~15% of global REE production. NdPr separation at Mountain Pass being scaled. Fort Worth magnet plant producing. 10X facility (10,000 t/yr NdFeB) planned.

name: Lynas Rare Earths — Mount Weld Mine
why_it_matters: World's highest-grade rare earth deposit (14.8% TREO) and largest non-China REE mine; 35+ year mine life supports Lynas Malaysia processing plant; critical allied-bloc source of NdPr for EV/AI magnets.

capacity.notes: Expansion underway targeting 12,000 MT/yr NdPr. Kalgoorlie facility now produces MREC feedstock shipped to Malaysia.

name: Lynas Advanced Materials Plant (LAMP) — Kuantan
why_it_matters: World's largest rare earth processing plant outside China; 10,500 MT/yr NdPr separation capacity; sole large-scale non-Chinese source of separated heavy rare earths (Dy, Tb) in allied bloc.

capacity.notes: Upgraded in late 2023. Adds Dy and Tb separation capability from 2025. Also produces La, Ce, Sm, Eu etc.

name: Masan High-Tech Materials — Nui Phao Mine
why_it_matters: One of the world's largest tungsten deposits outside China; Vietnam is #2 global tungsten producer; refinery capacity 6,500 MT/yr APT — a critical allied-bloc chokepoint as China controls 83% of supply and imposed export controls.

capacity.notes: Nui Phao produces almost all of Vietnam's ~3,400 MT/yr. Refinery capacity 6,500 MT APT/yr. Underground reserves approved for expansion.

name: Iceblick / Cryoin — Odesa Neon Purification
why_it_matters: Ukraine historically supplied 50-70% of global semiconductor-grade neon; Odesa facilities produce ultra-pure neon (99.9995%) essential for ArF/KrF excimer lasers in lithography; war disruptions caused 10-15× price spikes.

capacity.notes: Cryoin+Iceblick combined ~25,000-35,000 m³/month pre-war. Odesa operations partially resumed; capacity degraded. US fabs relied on Ukrainian neon for ~80-90% of imports.

name: JSR Corporation — Yokkaichi Plant (EUV Photoresist)
why_it_matters: JSR holds ~24% global photoresist market share and leads in EUV Metal Oxide Resist (MOR) commercialization critical for sub-3nm nodes; state-owned by Japan as strategic national asset; nationalization underscores chokepoint status.

capacity.notes: Primary EUV R&D and production hub. MOR (via Inpria acquisition) for EUV being commercialized. New Korea plant (JMK) for final MOR steps planned 2026.

name: Tokyo Ohka Kogyo (TOK) — Kawasaki HQ & Production
why_it_matters: Co-leader in EUV photoresists with JSR; TSMC accounts for ~34% of TOK revenue; world-leader in semiconductor photoresist since developing Japan's first in 1968; investing $134 M in new Koriyama factory.

capacity.notes: New state-of-the-art factory being built in Koriyama, Japan. Also expanding Korea presence. Co-developing MTR platform with Irresistible Materials for high-NA EUV.

name: Shin-Etsu Chemical — Naoetsu (Niigata) Photoresist Plant
why_it_matters: Primary photoresist production base for Shin-Etsu, one of three global EUV resist suppliers; produces ArF and EUV resists for leading-edge chipmakers worldwide; fourth plant now being built in Isesaki (Gunma) for further capacity.

capacity.notes: Naoetsu is primary site; Isesaki (Gunma) site under construction (4th base). Shin-Etsu is top tier supplier to TSMC, Samsung, Intel.

name: Sumitomo Chemical — Osaka Works (ArF/EUV Photoresist)
why_it_matters: World-leading ArF immersion photoresist technology with large global market share; capacity expanded 2.5× since 2019; EUV next-gen organic molecular resist in development; Osaka + Iksan (Korea) two-site supply model for BCP.

capacity.notes: Osaka Works primary site; Dongwoo Fine-Chem Iksan plant (Korea) added as second node in 2024. Capital investment in semiconductors ~100B yen over recent years.

name: CMC Materials (Cabot Microelectronics) — Aurora HQ & Plant
why_it_matters: World's leading CMP slurry supplier; critical planarization materials required at every metal/oxide layer in advanced logic/memory chip manufacturing; >80% sales offshore to Asia/Europe while R&D anchored in Aurora.

capacity.notes: ~1,000-5,000 employees globally. Acquired by Entegris in 2022; Fujifilm acquired electronic chemicals division from CMC Materials/Entegris for $700M in 2023.

name: Fujimi Incorporated — Kiyosu (Aichi) CMP Slurry
why_it_matters: Leading Japanese CMP slurry supplier for oxide, tungsten, Cu, and SiC wafer polishing; US SiC slurry plant (Tualatin, OR) for EV power semiconductor supply; critical to CMP steps at all advanced nodes.

capacity.notes: HVM SiC slurry manufacturing established at Tualatin, Oregon USA in 2019 for power semiconductor market.

process_or_product: PLANERLITE CMP slurries (oxide, W, Cu, poly-Si)

name: SK Specialty (SK Materials) — Sangju NF3 Plant
why_it_matters: World's #1 producer of NF3 (chamber cleaning gas) with >40% global market share and 12,000 MT/yr capacity; also #1 in WF6 (tungsten deposition gas); supplies TSMC, Samsung, and all leading fabs globally.

capacity.notes: Domestic production plus 1,500 MT/yr NF3 at China subsidiary. Also #1 WF6, #2 SiH4 globally.

name: Kanto Denka Kogyo — Mizushima NF3/SF6 Plant
why_it_matters: Major global NF3 producer; 2025 plant fire disrupted supply and triggered alerts at TSMC and Rapidus, demonstrating how concentrated specialty gas supply creates acute chokepoints even within allied nations.

capacity.notes: Exact capacity not public. Fire at NF3 operations in August 2025 disrupted supply and alarmed chipmakers including TSMC. One of top 3 global NF3 producers.

name: Linde Electronics — Bridgewater NJ (Global HQ) / Medford OR
sub_type: Ultra-high-purity specialty gases (rare gases, laser mixtures, fluorine)
why_it_matters: Global leader in rare gas and laser mixture production technology for lithography; supplies ultra-pure neon, ArF/KrF laser gases, and fluorine at semiconductor fabs worldwide including Samsung Pyeongtaek (8 on-site ASUs).

capacity.unit: global rank in electronics specialty gases
capacity.notes: Operates global network of electronics gas plants in key semiconductor hubs. Pyeongtaek (Samsung) is Linde's single largest electronics customer site worldwide.

name: Stella Chemifa — Kitakyushu HF Plant
why_it_matters: Top-tier supplier of semiconductor-grade HF (12N+ purity); indispensable for wafer etching and cleaning at 5nm/3nm nodes; leading market share in Japan and exports to Korea/Taiwan; critical chokepoint in wet chemical supply.

capacity.notes: Production capacity surpassed 100,000 MT/yr across two domestic Japan locations and one overseas. 12N+ purity achieved.

name: JX Advanced Metals — Isohara Works (Sputter Targets)
parent_company: JX Advanced Metals Corporation (JX Holdings group)
sub_type: Semiconductor sputter targets (Cu, Ti, Ta, W, Co)
why_it_matters: Controls ~60% of global semiconductor sputter target market; produces 6N copper, 5N titanium, 4N5 tantalum targets for interconnect layers in all advanced logic and memory chips; sole integrated upstream-to-target copper producer.

capacity.notes: Three overseas processing plants (US, South Korea, Taiwan). Upstream: 6N Cu refined at Isohara. Also expanding InP substrate capacity 20%.

name: Materion Corporation — Newton MA (Sputter Targets)
why_it_matters: Broadest portfolio of precious and non-precious metal sputter targets globally; key supplier for advanced logic gate electrodes, tantalum barriers (via Dangjin Korea), and gold targets for power/RF semiconductors.

capacity.unit: primary sputter target sites (Newton MA + Dangjin South Korea)
capacity.notes: Newton is source material site; Dangjin Korea is fully finished tantalum target production.

name: Fujifilm Electronic Materials — Kumamoto CMP/Chemicals
why_it_matters: Newest Fujifilm semiconductor materials site in Japan's 'Silicon Island' Kyushu cluster; produces CMP slurries and process chemicals co-located with TSMC Japan fab; proximity strategy to serve TSMC Kumamoto.

capacity.unit: global CMP slurry sites (Kumamoto + AZ + Hsinchu + Tainan + Cheonan + Belgium 2026)
capacity.notes: Kumamoto opened January 2024 to serve TSMC Japan. New Belgium site (Zwijndrecht) due spring 2026.

name: Shin-Etsu Handotai — Shirakawa Plant
why_it_matters: SEH's flagship 300mm wafer plant; alone produces ~20% of global semiconductor wafer supply; its 2011 earthquake shutdown was responsible for a 25% global supply disruption, illustrating its singular importance.

capacity.unit: % global silicon wafer supply (SEH Shirakawa alone)
capacity.notes: SEH is #1 or #2 globally in wafer market. Shirakawa is the world's largest single wafer fab. SEH group also has plants in Malaysia and US (SEH America, Vancouver WA).

key_customers: all leading logic and memory fabs

name: S.E.H. Malaysia — Shah Alam & Ulu Klang Plants
operator: S.E.H. Malaysia Sdn. Bhd. / S.E.H. (Shah Alam) Sdn. Bhd.
parent_company: Shin-Etsu Handotai / Shin-Etsu Chemical Co., Ltd.
why_it_matters: Key SEH production hub in Southeast Asia providing supply diversification; processes 200mm and 300mm wafers for global fab customers; part of SEH's multi-geography strategy to reduce concentration in Japan.

capacity.notes: Two separate facilities in Shah Alam and Ulu Klang. SEH America (Vancouver WA) is third geography.

name: SUMCO — Kyushu Factory (Imari / Nagahama / Saga)
why_it_matters: SUMCO's flagship 300mm wafer complex; SUMCO is the world's #2 pure-play silicon wafer company serving 200+ customers; Kyushu is primary hub for leading-edge wafer supply to TSMC, Samsung, and memory fabs.

capacity.notes: Also has Noda Office, Yonezawa Plant (ingot growth), SUMCO Phoenix (US, AZ+Phoenix), PT SUMCO Indonesia, and Formosa SUMCO Taiwan.

name: SUMCO — Yonezawa Plant (Ingot Growth)
why_it_matters: Dedicated ingot growth facility feeding SUMCO's wafer polishing lines; Czochralski crystal pulling at scale is a critical upstream step directly dependent on Spruce Pine HPQ; concentration of ingot growth in a few Japan plants creates supply risk.

capacity.notes: Primary ingot growth plant for SUMCO Kyushu. Noda Office handles wafer manufacture in same cluster.

name: GlobalWafers America — Sherman Texas Wafer Fab
why_it_matters: First advanced 300mm silicon wafer fab built in the US in over 20 years; GlobalWafers' most advanced fully integrated facility at $3.5 B; critical for onshoring wafer supply for TSMC Arizona and Intel Ohio fabs.

capacity.notes: Phase I opened May 2025. Phase II (additional $4B) in planning. Full build-out 142 acres, 6 phases. Current ~180 employees; target 650+ by 2028.

name: GlobalWafers — Hsinchu HQ & Taiwan Operations
why_it_matters: GlobalWafers global HQ and primary R&D center; Taiwan operations feed TSMC's massive domestic wafer demand; GlobalWafers operates 18 facilities across 9 nations making it #3 global wafer supplier by volume.

capacity.unit: production and operational facilities in 9 countries
capacity.notes: Taiwan is primary hub. European site at Novara, Italy also key. US expansion (Sherman TX) is flagship new investment.

name: Siltronic AG — Freiberg Plant
why_it_matters: Europe's primary silicon wafer production facility; key supplier to Infineon, TSMC Dresden, and other European fabs; Siltronic is #4 global wafer producer by volume; Freiberg anchors Europe's semiconductor material self-sufficiency.

capacity.notes: High-volume 300mm production. Siltronic's other major sites are in Singapore (largest) and Portland/Burghausen (Wacker joint history).

name: Siltronic Silicon Wafer Pte. Ltd. — Singapore
why_it_matters: Siltronic's largest and most advanced production hub; Singapore fabs among the world's newest and largest 300mm plants; $3 B facility expansion in Singapore; critical for East Asian wafer supply to TSMC and leading fabs.

capacity.notes: Largest production capacity in Siltronic network. Dedicated 300mm and 200mm lines. Among the most modern worldwide.

name: SK Siltron — Gumi (South Korea) 300mm Wafer Fab
why_it_matters: SK Group's integrated wafer subsidiary; >1.2 million 300mm wafers/month capacity; leverages SK Group vertical integration (raw material supply from SK affiliates); primary wafer supplier to Samsung and SK Hynix.

capacity.notes: Gumi is primary 300mm production site. Group also has SK Siltron CSS (Michigan, SiC) and China operations.

name: SK Siltron CSS — Bay City, Michigan (SiC Wafers)
why_it_matters: Projected top-5 global SiC wafer producer; SiC wafers are critical for EV power electronics and AI data center power delivery ICs; DOE-backed facility providing allied-bloc SiC independence from Chinese producers.

capacity.unit: projected global rank for SiC wafers
capacity.notes: Michigan facility formerly DuPont Compound Semiconductor / Dow Corning SiC assets. DOE Energy Department profile confirms top-5 global position projection.

name: OCI Tokuyama Semiconductor Materials (OTSM) — Bintulu, Sarawak
operator: OCI Tokuyama Semiconductor Materials Sdn. Bhd. (OTSM)
why_it_matters: Southeast Asia's first semiconductor-grade polysilicon facility; 8,000 MT/yr of 11N poly powered by Sarawak hydropower; $435 M JV diversifies supply away from China; IFC/World Bank-backed, signaling strategic importance.

capacity.notes: Pilot ops 2027; full commercial production 2029. Powered by Bakun Dam hydroelectric. Samalaju Industrial Park site.

name: Northern Rare Earth Group — Baotou Operations
operator: Inner Mongolia Baotou Steel Rare-Earth (Group) Hi-Tech Co.
sub_type: Light rare earth mining and separation
why_it_matters: World's largest single rare earth producer; Bayan Obo deposit (~40% of global REE reserves) gives China structural leverage over NdPr for AI-era permanent magnets; April 2025 export controls on 7 REEs directly weaponize this dependency.

capacity.notes: China total controls ~60% of global REE production. Northern RE Group is the largest single entity. April 2025 export controls on Nd, Pr, Dy, Tb, etc. sent global shock.

name: Air Liquide Electronics — Global Specialty Gas Network
why_it_matters: Major global supplier of ultra-high-purity N2, O2, Ar, H2, and specialty gases to semiconductor fabs; alongside Linde and Air Products forms the oligopoly supplying all critical fab gases; on-site ASU model embeds suppliers into fabs.

capacity.notes: Key semiconductor gas hubs in Dalian (China), Sasolburg (S Africa for rare gases), and major on-site facilities at TSMC, Samsung, and Intel sites globally.

name: Formosa SUMCO Technology — Taiwan (Hsinchu/Tongluo)
why_it_matters: Key Taiwan-based 300mm wafer supplier via SUMCO JV; directly co-located within Taiwan's semiconductor cluster serving TSMC; Formosa Plastics' upstream integration adds supply resilience.

capacity.notes: Located at Tongluo Science Park. Produces monocrystalline ingots and polished/epi wafers. Part of SUMCO's global 300mm network.

name: Lynas Rare Earths — Kalgoorlie Processing Facility
why_it_matters: First stage of Lynas' strategy to move cracking/leaching to Australia (closer to Mount Weld mine) and reduce Malaysia regulatory risk; MREC dispatched to LAMP for final separation; adds resilience to non-Chinese REE supply chain.

capacity.notes: Commenced production and first MREC shipment to Malaysia Q2 2024. Ramping up alongside Mt Weld expansion to eventually support 12,000 tpa NdPr capacity.

process_or_product: cracking and leaching of REE concentrate

name: SUMCO Phoenix Corporation — Phoenix & Albuquerque Plants
why_it_matters: SUMCO's primary US wafer supply hub; serves Intel Arizona, TSMC Arizona, and US-based memory fabs; key for domestic supply resilience as TSMC Arizona ramps to 2nm; two Arizona/New Mexico sites provide BCP redundancy.

capacity.notes: SPX Phoenix: ingot growth + wafer polishing. SPX Albuquerque: wafer polishing. Both ISO 14001 certified.

name: PT. SUMCO Indonesia — Batam
why_it_matters: SUMCO's Southeast Asian wafer operation provides supply diversification; Batam's Free Trade Zone proximity to Singapore enables rapid logistics to Singapore/Malaysia/Thailand fabs.

capacity.notes: Primarily 200mm silicon wafer manufacturing. ISO 14001 certified. Part of SUMCO's 9-country global network.

name: ASML Veldhoven HQ
why_it_matters: Sole producer of EUV and High-NA EUV lithography systems; absolute single point of failure for sub-7nm logic, HBM DRAM, and 3D NAND scaling.

capacity.notes: ASML shipped ~60 EUV units in 2023; High-NA ramp ongoing

name: ASML Wilton Campus
why_it_matters: ASML's largest US R&D and manufacturing site, specializing in mechatronics, alignment/sensor technology, and world-class optical fabrication critical to EUV performance.

capacity.notes: $200M expansion announced 2022 adding 1,000 jobs

name: ASML Cymer (San Diego)
why_it_matters: World's only manufacturer of DUV excimer laser light sources and EUV laser-produced plasma (LPP) light sources; without Cymer's droplet generators, no EUV wafers are produced globally.

capacity.notes: Also sole supplier of EUV LPP light source components

name: ASML Taiwan Service Hubs
why_it_matters: On-site maintenance and logistics hub for ASML EUV/DUV fleet at TSMC, UMC, and Micron Taiwan—uptime of these service engineers is co-equal with TSMC wafer output.

capacity.notes: Hsinchu, Taichung, and Tainan service clusters; >5,000 ASML customer support engineers globally

name: ASML Korea (Hwaseong Campus)
why_it_matters: Regional hub for servicing Samsung and SK Hynix EUV fleets; SK Hynix deployed first production High-NA EUV here in 2024, anchoring Korea's HBM ramp for AI accelerators.

capacity.notes: ₩240B new Hwaseong Campus opened late 2025 with repair and training centers

name: Carl Zeiss SMT (Oberkochen)
why_it_matters: Only manufacturer of the precision reflective optical columns for ASML EUV and High-NA EUV systems; no Zeiss optics means no EUV chip production worldwide.

capacity.notes: One optical column per ASML EUV system; capacity scales with ASML shipments

name: VDL ETG (Eindhoven)
why_it_matters: Sole assembler of the multi-meter precision structural frames that suspend and position Zeiss EUV mirrors inside ASML systems; a bottleneck shared with ASML itself.

capacity.notes: Co-capacity-constrained with ASML EUV output; also builds frames at VDL ETG Almelo and Switzerland

name: TRUMPF Ditzingen HQ
why_it_matters: Only producer of the CO₂ high-power pulsed laser amplifier that generates plasma to create EUV light inside every ASML EUV system; removal from supply chain halts all EUV production.

capacity.notes: One laser system per EUV scanner; TRUMPF's laser amplifier fires 50,000 pulses/second at tin droplets

name: Applied Materials Santa Clara HQ
why_it_matters: World's largest WFE company by revenue; dominates CVD, PVD, and CMP for advanced logic and HBM DRAM; its Endura and Producer platforms are on every leading-edge fab line.

capacity.notes: FY2024 record revenue; ~34,000 employees globally

name: Applied Materials Austin Campus
why_it_matters: Key US manufacturing and engineering hub for Applied Materials, supporting etch and deposition systems for advanced nodes and serving Intel and Texas fab ecosystem.

capacity.notes: Major US manufacturing campus; address 7803 Shafer Rd, Austin TX

name: Applied Materials Singapore
operator: Applied Materials Singapore Technology Pte. Ltd.
why_it_matters: Applied's largest manufacturing hub outside the US; 'Singapore 2030' plan targets doubling capacity, adding advanced packaging innovation, reinforcing AI supply chain resilience.

capacity.notes: Doubling to ~5,000 under Singapore 2030 plan; new factory employs ~1,000 once operational

name: Lam Research Fremont HQ
why_it_matters: Dominant in plasma etch (HAR etch for 3D NAND/HBM) and deposition; ~19,200 employees globally; its VECTOR and KIYO tools are mission-critical for all advanced memory and logic.

capacity.notes: FY2024 ~$14.9B revenue; 17,450 employees at FY2024 end

name: Lam Research Tualatin Campus
why_it_matters: Key Lam etch R&D and manufacturing hub co-located near Intel and industry partners in the Portland semiconductor corridor.

capacity.notes: Major R&D and manufacturing campus

name: Lam Research Penang (Batu Kawan)
why_it_matters: Lam's largest manufacturing facility globally, opened 2021 in Batu Kawan; central to building out HBM etch capacity for AI memory demand.

capacity.notes: State-of-the-art facility described as largest in Lam's network

name: Tokyo Electron HQ (Akasaka Biz Tower)
why_it_matters: Japan's largest WFE maker; sole global supplier of photoresist coater/developers (CLEAN TRACK) used at every leading fab for EUV layers—no TEL means no EUV patterning.

capacity.notes: FY2024 approx; >10,000 employees globally

name: Tokyo Electron Kyushu (Koshi, Kumamoto)
why_it_matters: Primary manufacturing site for TEL CLEAN TRACK coater/developer systems and advanced 3D packaging wafer bonders; new ¥47B Process Development Building completed October 2025.

capacity.notes: New ¥47B (~$312M) development building completed Oct 2025 for coaters, cleaners, wafer bonders

name: TEL Technology Solutions (Nirasaki, Yamanashi)
why_it_matters: TEL's primary R&D hub for thermal processing, deposition, and etch systems; new 21,000 m² development building completed mid-2023 for advanced deposition and patterning R&D.

capacity.notes: New dev building completed June 2023 for ALD, CVD, etch and patterning R&D

name: KLA Milpitas HQ
why_it_matters: Global leader in semiconductor process control and inspection; KLA tools are the last line of defense against yield loss—every advanced logic and HBM fab depends on KLA inspection.

capacity.notes: FY2024; ~12,600 employees globally

name: KLA Migdal HaEmek (Israel R&D)
why_it_matters: Birthplace of the Archer overlay metrology series—the industry standard for 30+ years; also the global hub for KLA's robotic arm platform shared across all products.

capacity.notes: Major R&D and manufacturing hub in Ramat Gavriel Industrial Zone

name: KLA Ann Arbor R&D Center
why_it_matters: KLA's second US headquarters and dedicated R&D center for automotive and advanced semiconductor process control; $70M+ investment leveraging University of Michigan talent.

capacity.notes: $70M+ investment; serves as KLA's second US HQ

name: SCREEN Holdings Hikone Plant
why_it_matters: SCREEN is the dominant supplier of single-wafer wet cleaning systems globally; its Hikone plants produce tools used in virtually every advanced logic and memory fab clean step.

capacity.notes: New S3-5 facility completed 2024 adding ~$107M in capacity; S3-4 still under construction

name: Hitachi High-Tech Naka (Etch / CD-SEM)
why_it_matters: Hitachi High-Tech's CD-SEM tools (CG-series) are the leading-edge industry standard for critical dimension measurement at sub-5nm; also a key etch systems supplier.

capacity.notes: New Kudamatsu etch plant added 35,000 m² in March 2025, doubling etch production capacity

name: Nikon Kumagaya Lithography Plant
why_it_matters: Primary manufacturer of Nikon DUV steppers/scanners; while trailing ASML at leading edge, Nikon systems serve critical mid-node capacity (power devices, analog, automotive) globally.

capacity.notes: Nikon has >8,000 lithography systems installed worldwide; Kumagaya is dev/design/mass production site

name: ASM International North America HQ (Scottsdale)
why_it_matters: ASM's global ALD market leadership is anchored in Arizona; new €300M 250,000 sq ft North America HQ under development (completion ~2026) for next-gen ALD R&D critical to gate-all-around transistors.

capacity.notes: €300M (~$320M) investment; >800 current employees scaling to ~1,300

name: Advantest Gunma (Ota) Factory
why_it_matters: World's largest ATE maker by revenue; Gunma is the primary manufacturing base for V93000 SoC and T2000 memory testers used in every AI chip and HBM production line.

capacity.notes: FY2023/2024 revenue; ~7,600 employees; Gunma Factory built 1984

name: Teradyne North Reading HQ
why_it_matters: Second-largest ATE maker globally; UltraFLEX and J750 testers are critical for logic and mixed-signal chip qualification; leadership in AI chip test development services.

capacity.notes: Annual revenue; industry duopoly with Advantest in advanced SoC test

name: Onto Innovation (Wilmington, MA)
why_it_matters: Leader in advanced packaging inspection and 3D bump metrology for HBM and AI chiplet assemblies; $240M+ HBM volume purchase agreement with leading memory maker through 2027.

capacity.notes: Formed 2019 from merger of Rudolph Technologies and Nanometrics; NYSE: ONTO

name: Synopsys Mountain View HQ
why_it_matters: Largest EDA company by revenue; Synopsys Fusion Compiler and IC Validator are the dominant sign-off tools for every advanced AI chip design; 2024 acquisition of Ansys adds simulation.

capacity.notes: Includes Ansys post-acquisition; EDA market duopoly with Cadence

name: Synopsys Hyderabad
why_it_matters: Major Synopsys engineering hub in India driving EDA software development and design verification at competitive cost; key for IP development and customer support across Asia.
